metadata.summary: VUI testing validates voice interfaces across three accuracy tiers (ASR word-error rate, NLU intent+slot F1, dialog task completion) using a two-tier suite: deterministic scripted-fixture assertions and LLM-judge scoring for naturalness and repair quality. The method covers corpus construction, noise-injection stress testing, metric tracking, and CI integration.

content.title: VUI Testing Best Practices
content.summary: VUI testing validates voice interfaces across three accuracy tiers (ASR word-error rate, NLU intent+slot F1, dialog task completion) using a two-tier suite: deterministic scripted-fixture assertions and LLM-judge scoring for naturalness and repair quality. The method covers corpus construction, noise-injection stress testing, metric tracking, and CI integration.

why.p: Voice interfaces fail in ways that standard usability testing misses: ASR provider upgrades shift accuracy silently, no-input and barge-in edge cases break state machines, and naturalness degrades invisibly when prompts are repeated verbatim on retry. Separating test tiers (deterministic vs LLM-judge) and tracking ASR, NLU, and dialog completion independently isolates where regressions originate.

list.item: Pre-launch validation of a voice skill or custom agent: stress-test intent recognition and dialog flow.
Multilingual/accent expansion: regression-test ASR accuracy and prompt naturalness across locales.
Continuous regression: every NLU model or prompt change re-runs utterance fixture corpus.
Field testing prep: scripted noise-injection runs before recruiting real users.
Voice is a minor shortcut (tap-to-speak search) — standard usability testing covers it.
No working dialog model yet — start with Wizard-of-Oz prototyping.
Single-turn command testing only — an NLU benchmark (intent classification F1) is sufficient.

section.name: testing-layers
section.p: Five layers in order of granularity: unit (intent recognition accuracy), integration (dialog flow completion), user (real-world usability), stress (noise, interruptions, edge cases), accessibility (accent diversity, speech impairment).

rule.statement: Track three accuracy tiers separately: ASR word-error rate, NLU intent+slot F1, and dialog task completion. A regression in only one tier tells you exactly where to look.
rule.rationale: Aggregating across tiers hides the root cause. ASR regression means transcription changed; NLU regression means intent model changed; dialog regression means state machine changed. Each has a different fix.

list.item: Intent accuracy: % correct intent detection per utterance
Task completion: % of users completing their goal end-to-end
Error rate: dialog failures per conversation
Word error rate (WER): ASR transcription accuracy
User satisfaction: post-interaction survey (CSAT or 1-5 scale)

rule.statement: Run a two-tier test suite: tier 1 = deterministic assertions over scripted utterance fixtures (intent, slot, dialog-state outcomes); tier 2 = LLM-judge scoring for naturalness, error recovery, and goal completion against a rubric. Never mix the two tiers' outputs into a single pass/fail count.
rule.rationale: Deterministic assertions catch regressions objectively. LLM-judge scoring evaluates qualitative properties that assertions cannot reach. Mixing them produces an opaque aggregate that hides which type of regression occurred.

rule.statement: Pin ASR model versions in CI. Provider-side ASR upgrades silently shift WER and break tests overnight.
rule.rationale: Cloud ASR providers update models without notice. A passing suite built against model v1 can fail entirely against model v2 with no code changes. Version-pin prevents silent shifts.

rule.statement: Build the utterance corpus from real anonymized user logs first; synthesize LLM variants second. Pure LLM-generated corpora over-represent grammatical, in-domain phrasings and miss real-user degradation.
rule.rationale: Real logs capture the messy, elliptical, code-switched, and misspelled phrasings that actual users produce. LLMs trained on clean text generate clean test cases that produce falsely high accuracy numbers.

rule.statement: Include barge-in tests (user interrupts mid-prompt) and no-input tests (timeout) in every suite. These break dialog state machines more reliably than utterance variation.
rule.rationale: Barge-in and timeout edge cases break state machines at the interrupt handler level, independent of intent recognition. They are structurally different failures requiring different fixes.

section.name: noise-injection

rule.statement: Mix clean utterances with ambient noise (TV, traffic, cafe) at multiple SNR levels (20dB, 10dB, 5dB) for stress testing. Use ffmpeg for mixing.
rule.rationale: Synthetic noise injection is imperfect (misses room acoustics and beamforming) but captures most recognizable degradation modes: background speech, low SNR, frequency masking.

section.reference: Shell script: mix utterances with noise at 3 SNR levels

rule.statement: Tier the suite: smoke (10 utterances, every commit) → full (nightly) → field-replay (release). Do not run the full LLM-judge sweep on every commit — cost and latency are non-trivial.
rule.rationale: A 1000-utterance x 3-noise x 2-locale x LLM-judge sweep is expensive. Tiering keeps commit feedback fast while ensuring thorough coverage before release.

rule.statement: Run the same suite in at least two locales even for monolingual launches. Reveals locale-leaking copy and fallback bugs.
rule.rationale: Locale bugs appear at locale boundaries: hardcoded English fallbacks, date/number format assumptions, and ASR models with uneven locale coverage. Single-locale suites miss all of these.

antipattern.description: LLM judge sees the expected answer when scoring naturalness — anchoring bias inflates scores.
antipattern.reason: Score naturalness blind, then compute correctness separately. Judges with access to expected answers rate responses as more natural when they are also correct, masking naturalness failures in correct responses.

antipattern.description: Using absolute 1-5 naturalness scores instead of pairwise comparison.
antipattern.reason: Absolute LLM scores are unreliable across sessions. Pairwise comparison ("Is A or B more natural?") produces more consistent results and surfaces meaningful differences between prompt variants.

antipattern.description: Using the same TTS vendor for test fixtures as for product output — produces false-high naturalness scores.
antipattern.reason: Same-vendor TTS creates circular evaluation: the LLM judge finds familiar prosody patterns more natural. Use a different TTS engine for fixture generation than for product rendering.

antipattern.description: Sending raw user-recorded audio to a cloud LLM judge.
antipattern.reason: Raw audio may contain PII, bystander voices, and background conversations. Always transcribe locally first, redact PII, then judge the text. Never pass raw audio to an external model.

section.name: infrastructure-antipatterns

antipattern.description: Running the full LLM-judge suite concurrently without concurrency caps, hitting 429 rate limits that cause silent test passes.
antipattern.reason: 429 responses can be swallowed as empty results rather than errors. Cap concurrency at provider rate limits and treat any 429 as a test failure, not a skip.

section.reference: Per-turn LLM judge prompt scoring intent_match, naturalness, brevity, error_recovery

template.purpose: VUI test plan: objectives, fixture inventory, metric targets, CI tier schedule.
Shell script: mix clean utterances with ambient-noise stems at multiple SNR levels.
Per-turn LLM judge prompt scoring intent_match, naturalness, brevity, error_recovery.

checklist.check: ASR word-error rate is tracked separately from NLU and dialog metrics.
Utterance corpus is built from real anonymized user logs first.
Corpus includes barge-in tests (user interrupts mid-prompt).
Noise injection covers at least three SNR levels (20dB, 10dB, 5dB).
Suite is tiered: smoke (every commit), full (nightly), field-replay (release).
Same suite runs in at least two locales.
LLM-judge tier is blind to expected answers when scoring naturalness.
TTS vendor for fixtures differs from product output vendor.
Concurrency caps are set to prevent rate-limit hits.
